An interesting volume in the collection is Davis’ copy of the ¢ Dub-
jin Magazine’® for 1843, containing, as is well known, his own valu-
able papers on the Irish Parliament of James II. Davis also appears
to have been much interested in Hudson’s papers in the same . maga~

had them extracted, bound in a substantial volume, and by carefully

large paper copy of J. R. O’ I‘lanagm.u s ‘““The Blackwater in Mun-
ster,” Lond., 1844, and it is w ell pencilled, several statements are cor-
rected, and several traditions recorded which he, as a Mallow man, had
leard, one to the cffect that Idmund Buwrke was born at Castletowm
Roche, and ‘‘ got drunk there more than once, when he had cut his
aisdom teeth.”

Other volumes bearing the autograph of Davis, but without mar-
ginal notes, are Fitzgerald and McGregor’s ‘‘ History of the City and:
County of Limerick,”” Harris’ ‘‘ Dublin,” D’Alton’s * Co. Dublin,’?
D'Alton’s *“ Annals of Boyle,”” Walker’s ¢ Irish Bards,” Plowden’s
“Treland from Union to 1810,° McNevin’s ¢ State Trials,” ete.

The bound volumes of pamphlets relating to Ireland are sixteenr im
number, each containing about fcurteen or fifteen pamphlets. They
evidé-nrbly had been bound under Davis’s directions, and in two cases
the slip containing his instructions to the binder as to the lettering
still remain within the volume. They are arranged according to sub-
jects—Irish Trade, Irish Agriculture, Irish Education, Statistics, Poor
Laws, Roman Catholic (Guestions, United Irishmen and Defenders,
Union and Anti-Union, Miscellaneous, etc. The earliest is dated 1736,
the latest 1844, the year before his death.

for Down, of the Umited Irishmen, in 1796. On the eve of the omi-
break of hostilities in ’minety-eight, he was arrested and removed tq
the Provost, in Belfast, from which he was sent to Fort George, in
Scotland, as a State prisomer. Here he was detained for over th.ree
years. Omn his release and return to Ireland, in 1802, he was unjustly
treated by some of his co-ministers, especially Robert Black, who wag
under the Castlereagh influence, and the presbyterian state bounty
enjoved by the others was denied to him.  He ministered at Keady, Co.
Armagh, from 1803 until 1815, when he resigned through ill-health.
He was suspended in 1812 for publishing his ‘‘ Narrative.”

The -sympathetic pen of the Rev. Henry Montgomery, D.D., himself '
the son of one who was ** burred out” in '08, thus describes his clos-
ing years: ¢ Finally, oppressed with age and sorrow, he was obliged
to retire from the ministry, and for several years he enjoyed the
gratuitous shelter of an humble roof in the suburbs of Belfast.

There . . this good and amiable man, ‘majestic though m
ruins,” closed his earthly pilgrimage. From the year 1780 until the
year 1798, he was a courted and an honoured guest in the most splen-
did mansions of Belfiast,. where alternately his patriotic wisdom com-
manded acquiescence, and his sparkling wit ‘ set the table in a roar.”
Yet without one stain upon his character I saw the earthly remains of
that .-gréaa:t man, even in the same town of Belfast, deposited mm a
pauper’s grave, where not even a stone marks ‘the marrow house of
his repose.” Some eight or ten individuals formed the emntire funeral
procession. The late W. D. H. MacEwen pronounced a pathetic ora-
dion.; and we left the melancholy spot moralising on the value of public
gratitude, the permancncy of political friendships,-and the value of
popular applause.’
